coredata.dc:title: Improving the oxygen barrier properties of composite films using green tea extracted CQDs and PVA for active packaging applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ansari, Jamilur R.
Park, Kitae
Seo, Jongchul
coredata.dc:description: Smart food packaging extends the shelf life and preserves the food quality by adding functional agents that make it more appealing. Here, we have used a solvent casting technique to create flexible, transparent, and re-emissive thin films made of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and green tea extracts carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The composite films of CQDs/PVA can block UV rays by absorbing them and then re-emitting them in the visible spectrum. Additionally, these films also exhibit a powerful fluorescence emission. The composite film’s capacity to block UV light, hydrophilicity, and oxygen barrier properties was enhanced with varying experimental conditions. We have carried out a comprehensive study to investigate the potential mechanism of the resulting films and how they absorb and re-emit UV radiation. The CQDs/PVA composite films have excellent oxygen barrier properties that can prolong the shelf life of products, keeping them fresh and maintaining their quality. By optimizing the experimental conditions of CQDs in the PVA matrix, the hydrophilicity, and OTR of the composite films were significantly reduced from 40.36 ± 1.41° to 16.23 ± 0.20° and 6.39 ± 0.64 cc/m2·day to 0.34 ± 0.03 cc/m2·day respectively. Water resistance characteristics demonstrate effective H-bonding between CQDs and the PVA matrix, reducing the hydrophilicity of the PVA. The results of our study showed that CQDs have the potential to be utilized as oxygen barriers, UV-blocking coating materials, to make coatings and effective packaging, and to be used in the food industry, pharmaceutical storage, and medical sectors, among other applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green tea
Carbon quantum dots
UV-blocking
Oxygen transmission rate
Functional coatings
Fluorescent

UV-Vis and transparency study of CQDs(C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
Mechanical properties of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
Structural and thermal analysis of CQDs with PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film
Thermal study of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis of CQDs
Surface wettability of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
Oxygen transmission rate (OTR) analysis of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
Water resistance properties of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their gratitude to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) for providing financial support through the Brain Pool Fellowship to Jamilur R. Ansari (Grant No.: RS-2023\u201300263418). The authors also acknowledge Yonsei University, Sinchon Campus (South Korea) for conducting Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) imaging.

The authors extend their gratitude to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) for providing financial support through the Brain Pool Fellowship to Jamilur R. Ansari (Grant No.: RS-2023-00263418). The authors also acknowledge Yonsei University, Sinchon Campus (South Korea) for conducting Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) imaging.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-Vis absorption and (inset) transmittance spectra and their emission under UV light (b) photographic images showing the transparency of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film.
Mechanical properties of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film (a) tensile stress-strain curve (b) tensile strength vs elongation chart.
FTIR spectra of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film.
TGA of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film.
(a, b) DSC heating and cooling thermogram of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film.
Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQDs (C1-C4) and inset (UV-Vis emission).
TEM/HRTEM image and inset (histogram) of CQDs (C1-C4).
Water Contact Angle of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film.
Oxygen transmission rates of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film.
Percentage of swelling, and weight loss in CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film.
Schematic illustration shows the stepwise process for the preparation of CQDs/PVA film and their HRTEM image.
DSC heating and cooling thermogram of CQDs/PVA(C1-C4) and PVA film.

head.ce:title: Improving the oxygen barrier properties of composite films using green tea extracted CQDs and PVA for active packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Packaging & Logistics, Yonsei University, 1 Yonseidae-gil, Wonju-si, Gangwon-do 26493, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Packaging & Logistics, Yonsei University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Packaging & Logistics, Yonsei University, 1 Yonseidae-gil, Wonju-si, Gangwon-do 26493, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Smart food packaging extends the shelf life and preserves the food quality by adding functional agents that make it more appealing. Here, we have used a solvent casting technique to create flexible, transparent, and re-emissive thin films made of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and green tea extracts carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The composite films of CQDs/PVA can block UV rays by absorbing them and then re-emitting them in the visible spectrum. Additionally, these films also exhibit a powerful fluorescence emission. The composite film’s capacity to block UV light, hydrophilicity, and oxygen barrier properties was enhanced with varying experimental conditions. We have carried out a comprehensive study to investigate the potential mechanism of the resulting films and how they absorb and re-emit UV radiation. The CQDs/PVA composite films have excellent oxygen barrier properties that can prolong the shelf life of products, keeping them fresh and maintaining their quality. By optimizing the experimental conditions of CQDs in the PVA matrix, the hydrophilicity, and OTR of the composite films were significantly reduced from 40.36 ± 1.41° to 16.23 ± 0.20° and 6.39 ± 0.64 cc/m2·day to 0.34 ± 0.03 cc/m2·day respectively. Water resistance characteristics demonstrate effective H-bonding between CQDs and the PVA matrix, reducing the hydrophilicity of the PVA. The results of our study showed that CQDs have the potential to be utilized as oxygen barriers, UV-blocking coating materials, to make coatings and effective packaging, and to be used in the food industry, pharmaceutical storage, and medical sectors, among other applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Preserving the food’s quality and freshness, ensuring food safety, and extending the food’s shelf life are the three main concerns when packaging fresh food, particularly fruits and vegetables (Gaikwad et al., 2020). Fresh products with short shelf lives are the most common source of complaints from customers (Giménez et al., 2012). To preserve product quality, guarantee safety, and increase shelf life, smart and active packaging has gained momentum in the food packaging and supply chain in recent years (D. Kumar et al., 2018). Presently, the most popular process for producing active packaging materials involves integrating active ingredients with basic polymers used in food products (Priyadarshi & Rhim, 2020). Functional nanomaterials and physiologically active natural plant extracts are frequently utilized to give active packaging materials functionality (Priyadarshi et al., 2022). Recently, there has been a lot of interest in a novel class of carbon materials called carbon quantum dots, or CQDs (Wen et al., 2023). Due to their desirable properties, which include high water solubility, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and an easy synthesis process, fluorescent CQDs are used as functional fillers in food packaging applications (Baslak et al., 2023; Nizam et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2017). CQDs are a fantastic material that has recently gained attention for its potential applications in active food packaging as a safe, environmentally friendly, and sustainable functional material (Ezati, Roy, et al., 2022). Furthermore, excellent functional qualities have recently been reported, including antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV protection (Ezati, Rhim, et al., 2022; H. Li, Huang, et al., 2018). Carbon dots have been produced using a variety of carbon sources; however, the production of CQDs should use straightforward, affordable, and ecologically friendly raw materials (Nuntahirun et al., 2023; Shaik et al., 2021).

Generally speaking, there is a common process for creating a multifunctional packaging film with the addition of functional nanofillers (Priyadarshi et al., 2021, 2022). Therefore, active fillers that serve as antioxidants will be a useful and affordable alternative in the food packaging sector (Kousheh et al., 2020). In addition, it is worth noting that the surface of CQDs can be modified to introduce active functional groups that can significantly improve the physical and chemical properties of the base polymer. This is achieved by creating interfacial interactions between CQDs and the polymer matrix while also enhancing the UV-blocking properties of the material.

Nevertheless, depending on the basic polymer and the intrinsic characteristics of CQD, the high hydrophilicity of CQDs may result in a decrease in the moisture barrier qualities of the packaging film when it is introduced to the polymer matrix (Ezati, Rhim, et al., 2022; Roy et al., 2021). In the world of packaging, there is a distinct preference for polymer materials that boast exceptional mechanical and gas barrier properties. Among the various biodegradable polymers available, Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) stands as a unique material with some exceptional qualities. PVA is a water-soluble polymer that features multiple active hydroxyl (OH) groups, making it an ideal choice for various packaging applications (Ma et al., 2016). The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a synthetic polymer that is edible, non-toxic, biodegradable, and environmentally friendly, for clinical use in humans (Ben Halima, 2016; Chong et al., 2013). Food product coating and packaging have utilized PVA as an additive component because of these characteristics, along with its strong film-forming ability, high mechanical properties, and superior oxygen barrier (Gómez-Aldapa et al., 2020; Moreira et al., 2020). However, when utilized in food packaging systems, PVA has drawbacks such as low UV protection, high moisture absorption, poor biological activity, and poor thermal stability. By combining organic and inorganic nanomaterials, functional nanocomposites can be created, which have been used to improve the polymer’s functionality and properties. This is made possible by the hydrogen bonding groups in the PVA structure (Alaş et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2019).

In this paper, we present a novel highly efficient green tea extracts carbon quantum dots (CQDs) - PVA nanocomposites for food packaging applications that are inexpensive, simple to prepare, quick, and straightforward to operate. Here, we describe a simple, one-step method for creating a range of photoluminescent polymer composite films. This is accomplished by embedding blue, fluorescence-emitting CQDs into a PVA polymer, which serves as a coating to protect fruits from UV light and prolong their shelf life. The preparation conditions for CQDs have been optimized, and the size has been regulated. PVA’s hydroxyl groups in each repeating unit can be used as reactive sites to combine with multifunctional compounds to create three-dimensional networks that strengthen the material’s resistance to water dissolution and enhance its mechanical and thermal stability. In comparison to the pure PVA film, the oxygen permeabilities of the hybrid films containing different sizes of CQDs were significantly suppressed by 1.52 % - 17.52 %. This indicates that by adjusting the experimental conditions within the PVA matrix, the permeation of oxygen into the films is slowed, thereby enhancing the film’s oxygen barrier properties. We have enhanced the UV blocking capability, hydrophilicity, and OTR values of the composite films by optimizing the experimental conditions in the PVA matrix. We have conducted a comprehensive analysis of the thermal, mechanical, and optical properties of composite films.

Only additional purification was intended for all the analytical grade chemicals that were utilized. Polyvinyl Alcohol, the polymer (MW = 85,000 g/mol), was acquired from Sigma Aldrich. The CQDs were made by thoroughly mixing green tea extract powder, which was purchased from Korea. Ultra-pure deionized (DI) water 18.2 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiment.

Characterization of synthesized CQDs and fabricated thin films was done using an alternative characterization method. Using a bar-type automatic film coating apparatus (KIPEA E&T Co. Ltd., Hwasung, Korea), the CQDs/PVA solution was casted on a glass plate. The cast film was then dried for an hour at 60 °C. The absorbance and transmittance spectra of CQDs/PVA composite films and liquid CQD solutions were measured with a Shimadzu double-beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectrum measurements of CQDs were made using a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu) that was excited at 350 nm with a 5 nm slit width. The measurements were taken over a range of 350/650 nm. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted using a TGA (SDT Q600) instrument, which was operated in a controlled environment of nitrogen (N2) gas. The sample was subjected to a gradual temperature rise of 10 °C per minute, and the resulting changes in its weight were carefully monitored and recorded. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CQDs solution were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100F JEOL) by placing a drop of the sample on the copper grid and allowing it to evaporate under ambient conditions at room temperature. After subjecting the PVA and CQDs/PVA composite films to UV light, we captured fluorescence images of the samples. The physical characteristics of the PVA and CQDs/PVA polymer films were meticulously analyzed using the Universal Tensile Strength Machine (UTM), a sophisticated and accurate equipment produced by Qmesys Co, located in South Korea. The UTM is capable of precisely measuring the tensile strength and other mechanical properties of these films, ensuring a comprehensive assessment of their performance. The UTM device was utilized to evaluate the tensile strength, elasticity, and other key mechanical parameters of the films under controlled conditions. The barrier properties were analyzed by Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) using OX-TRAN 702 equipment (MOCON, USA). With a thickness tester, the thicknesses of all the films were measured. The film samples were analyzed for water contact angles using a Phoenix 300 contact-angle goniometer (SEO Co., Ltd., Suwon, Korea) with the sessile drop method, employing 5 µL of the water at 25°C. Using IBM SPSS statistics 29.0, SPSS Inc., Illinois, USA, and Duncan's multiple range tests, we have evaluated the significant differences (p < 0.05) between the film properties. The sample characteristics were computed after obtaining three independent measurements, producing a mean ± standard deviation. To evaluate the level of swelling and solubility of the PVA/CQDs film, we cut the films into 3.0 × 3.0 cm strips and weighed them. After weighing (wd), the composite films were immersed in 50 mL of DI water for 24 hours at room temperature (RT). The samples were then taken out, and tissue paper was used to dry the film’s surface and remove traces of water. Next, the films were immediately weighed (ws). The amount of swelling is indicated by the weight difference between the pre-and post-immersion states, while any observed alterations in the film’s integrity or total dissolution point to the solubility of the film. Finally, the samples were dried at 60 °C for 10 h and weighed (wa). The sample’s weight loss and swelling percentages were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2) (R. Kumar et al., 2024).
(1)
Weight Loss percentage ( % )
=
w
d
−
w
a
w
d
×
100 %
(2)
Swelling percentage ( % )
=

× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of green tea extract carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: We have employed waste-based, environmentally friendly, and sustainable carbonaceous materials to create carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a UV-blocking agent (waste utilization approach). To create CQDs, kitchen green tea residue was used as a precursor to carbonaceous materials. In short, green tea was grinded with a mortar and pestle into a fine powder after being left to dry at room temperature. A silica crucible containing the required amount of fine powder was carbonized for two hours at 180 °C in a muffle furnace at ambient conditions. The DI water was then mixed with finely divided black carbon powder and stirred for 2 hours. It was then kept in a hydrothermal tube at 200° C for 5 hours. It was then kept at room temperature (RT) to cool and then filtered through filter paper and the resultant black-brownish mixture, was centrifuged for 20 minutes at 10,000 rpm. Subsequently, a 0.22 μm syringe filter was used to extract all the small, homogeneous CQDs from the brown-colored sample. We have optimized the synthesis of CQDs by varying the experimental conditions. We have used various approaches, like calcining the tea powder, hydrothermal, filtration and centrifuge (C1), hydrothermal, filtration and centrifuge (C2), hydrothermal, and filtration (C3) no treatment and filtration (C4) CQDs and addition of these in the liquid PVA to test the CQDs’ ability to block UV light and oxygen transmission rate in the solid state. The CQD aqueous solution was diluted to a specified volume and refrigerated for future work.

The process of creating PVA-based composite films involved utilizing a solution casting method, which is illustrated in Scheme 1. The first step was to add 11 g of PVA to a solution of 100 mL of DI water that contained 1 mL of CQDs. The solution was subjected to a temperature of 95 degrees Celsius and agitated vigorously for 60 minutes to achieve complete dissolution of PVA. After a thorough stirring for 60 minutes, the different CQDs (C1-C4) were evenly distributed in the solution. Afterward, the solution was casted onto a glass substrate (measuring 24 cm × 30 cm) using a bar-type automatic film coating apparatus from KIPEA E&T Co. Ltd., Hwasung, South Korea. After coating these plates were dried in an oven kept at a constant temperature of 60 0C for one hour, and the resulting composite films had an average thickness of 78 ± 5 µm.

This process effectively evaporated the solvent and left behind a uniform, dry film on the surface of the plates. After the films were dried, they were carefully removed from the glass plates. The samples were placed in a humidity chamber at a temperature of 25 0C and a relative humidity of 50 % for 72 hours in order to prepare them for the next round of testing. Each film was given a unique name based on the type of CQD solution used during its preparation. The films were named C1/PVA, C2/PVA, C3/PVA, C4/PVA, and Pure PVA, respectively, to help differentiate between them.

In this study, we synthesized CQDs using a hydrothermal approach. The CQDs were then mixed with PVA to form a film. We examined the barrier properties and structures of the synthesized CQDs using various analytical techniques such as UV-Vis spectroscopy, Photoluminescence (PL), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and Transmission Electron Microscopy/High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM/HRTEM). The results of these analyses provided valuable insights into the properties and structures of CQDs, which can be useful in various applications. We conducted a study on the mechanical properties of a material using UTM and TGA. Additionally, we analyzed the rate at which oxygen can pass through the material using OTR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-Vis and transparency study of CQDs(C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, we have fabricated CQDs/PVA composite films using nanomaterial-based CQDs as a UV-blocking agent by varying experimental conditions. Therefore, it is essential to know the absorption profiles of each of them individually as well as composites of them in both liquid and solid states before adding CQDs to PVA films. Fig. 1a displays the absorption spectra of CQDs (C1-C4) and pure PVA films. We observed that liquid CQDs displayed extremely prominent and intense absorption maxima at approximately 270 nm which indicates that the UV-active CQDs were a factor in the absorption of the CQDs solution. As a result, solid CQDs can be added to PVA composite films to act as a UV-blocking agent. We have made variations in the synthesis of CQDs (C1-C4) and the addition of these in the liquid PVA to test the CQDs’ ability to block UV light in the solid state. Solid thin films were then obtained in accordance with the experimental protocol. The effectiveness of the CQDs/PVA composite films in blocking UV radiation was evaluated by measuring their transmittance (%) using the UV-Vis study, which involved conducting multiple experiments under different conditions. Researchers have discovered that the highest level of transmission of UV light in PVA film in its original condition occurs in the range of 190–400 nm across the UV region (Grosch et al., 2015). The PVA film that was in perfect condition could not absorb UV light in the range (225–275 nm). However, with the incorporation of 1 wt% of CQDs into PVA film, a significant shift in the film’s ability to absorb UV light was observed, and CQDs/PVA film was able to decrease light transmission in the UV region (225–275 nm). Additionally, we also found that the UV blocking capacity was enhanced upon varying the experiment conditions of CQDs/PVA composite films. The film variations in CQDs with PVA were able to completely block (almost 100 %) of the UV light in the wavelength range 225–275 nm.

UV-blocking films and agents are becoming increasingly important in various applications. In order to achieve optimal results, transparency is a crucial factor to consider. To address this need, waste-derived fluorescent CQDs have been combined with PVA to create composite films that offer excellent UV-blocking capabilities while remaining incredibly transparent, flexible, and thin. The photographic representations of CQDs (C1-C4) with PVA are shown in Fig. 1b, providing a visual demonstration of the effective integration of these materials. The effectiveness of the composite films can be seen in photographic images, as they display remarkable transparency to visible light. Additionally, the transparency of PVA films is found to remain unaffected even when the experimental conditions are altered. These observations serve to highlight the immense potential of such films in various applications requiring high transparency and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of the filler’s inclusion on the mechanical strength of PVA polymer and the CQDs was investigated through tensile tests. Fig. 2(a-b) illustrates how the tensile test determines the mechanical strength of the CQDs (C1-C4) with PVA composite films. Similar to PVA polymer film, the tensile curves of CQDs/PVA films exhibit elastic behavior brought about by stretching, and disentanglement (T. Li et al., 2019). The CQDs/PVA films’ tensile stress increases with the variations in the experiment conditions. For PVA film the tensile stress was nearly 10.7 MPa with a strain 107.92 %. These physical properties are essential in understanding the behavior of materials under stress and their ability to withstand external forces before breaking. The tensile stress rises from ∼4 to ∼29 MPa with varying experimental conditions. The increased stiffness of the film is the reason for the variation in tensile stress. The research suggests that the tensile strength of CQDs/PVA can be considerably improved by elevating the concentrations of CQDs. These CQDs combine with PVA and polymerize to form polystyrene, and also take part in non-covalent interactions (Peixoto et al., 2006). This process helps to enhance the overall strength of the material. Strong intermolecular interaction between the PVA and CQDs is expected to contribute to the improved mechanical properties of the various nanocomposites (each with different experiment conditions).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and thermal analysis of CQDs with PVA (C1-C4) and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectroscopy was used to evaluate the surface functionality and chemical makeup of materials at different stages of treatment. FTIR spectra of all the samples of CQDs (C1-C4) and pure PVA film are shown in Fig. 3. The analyzed samples were observed to exhibit two distinct peaks during the C-H stretch, which were identified as 599 and 2909 cm−1, and are a characteristic feature of the samples (Alaş et al., 2022). Furthermore, during the analysis, the -CH2 stretch peaks at 1326 cm−1 were also identified. Interestingly, these peaks were found to be stronger in the pure PVA samples and weaker in the polymer nanocomposites. Additionally, a peak at 2851 cm−1 was observed in the composites, which was negligible in the pure PVA sample (Zhao et al., 2017).

During the analysis of the pure PVA sample, it was observed that the sample exhibited stronger OH stretch peaks at 3265 and 1659 cm−1, C-C stretch peaks at 1417 cm−1, and C-O stretch peaks at 833, 1142, and 1376 cm−1 (Kassab et al., 2019; Lam et al., 2017). Conversely, these peaks were found to be weaker in the CQDs/PVA samples. It’s important to note that hydroxyl groups are attached to the carbon chain in the polymer chain of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). This means that there’s a high chance of interaction between the CQDs and the PVA polymer matrix. This interaction can have a significant impact on the properties and behavior of the resulting composite material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal study of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: One of the key characteristics of polymers that makes them useful for a variety of applications, particularly UV blocking, is their thermal stability. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is one of the most effective analytical methods for understanding the thermal properties and insights into the thermal stability, degradation, and composition of various materials. To conduct a thorough investigation of the effects of an additional UV blocking agent on the initial characteristics of PVA films, a thermal analysis was performed on CQDs/PVA aggregate films. The analysis was conducted under a nitrogen (N2) atmosphere to study the thermal properties of the films and determine the extent of their stability and durability. This study aimed to provide valuable insights into the potential applications of these films in various industrial and commercial sectors. The TGA analytical graphs of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA composite films are displayed in Fig. 4.

In general, the thermal analysis (TGA) graph shows that the degradation profile and thermal stability of these composite films show slight changes. As illustrated in Fig. 5, CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA film initially showed a similar profile up to 125 °C; consequently, there was no maximum weight loss (%) in any case. Minimal weight loss was noted for these composite films in the temperature range of 125 °C to 240 °C; the values were in the range ∼9.5 % and ∼10 % for these composite films. The water and other impurities with low boiling points are responsible for these losses. For all the composite films, a sharp drop in weight was seen between 240 and 460 degrees Celsius. The CQDs samples (C1-C4)/PVA film exhibit a percentage weight loss of approximately ∼16 % - 95 % in the second region. The decomposition process proceeds to 650 °C in the third region, where the films exhibit percentage losses in the range 9 %-16 %. Additionally, the sample C1 film shows the least weight loss in the temperature range of 350 °C to 800 °C, and its residual content is higher than that of the pure PVA film. This could be because the calcined CQDs that were added to the PVA film decompose over time.

Fig. 5(a-b) shows the DSC analyses of different CQDs/PVA composite films. We have carried out the DSC analysis for CQDs-(C1-C4)-PVA nanocomposite film to ascertain the degree to which the addition of CQDs affects the thermal transitions of PVA and to calculate the values of melting temperature (Tm) and crystalline temperature (Tc) for the nanocomposites. When CQDs are well dispersed in the PVA matrix, they function as nucleating agents, promoting the material’s crystalline nature and raising the crystallization peak temperature (Arya et al., 2024; Z. Li et al., 2015). Based on DSC analysis, Table 1 displays the Tm and Tc of the composite films containing pure PVA and CQDs/PVA nanocomposites. The DSC thermogram of the CQDs (C1-C4) in PVA aggregate composite films is shown in Fig. 5, where the plotting of heat flow (W/g) vs. temperature (°C) indicates the glass transition temperature of the composite films.

The degradation profile of CQDs(C1-C4)/PVA aggregate composite films was displayed by the DSC thermogram. The addition of CQDs to the PVA film resulted in a slight reduction in the degradation temperature of the CQDs/PVA composite film, which lowered from approximately 225 °C to 220 °C. However, the crystallization temperature of the composite film was observed to be around 195 °C. DSC heating and cooling thermogram of CQDs is shown in Table 1.

One of the main issues with using films for UV protection in traditional UV-blocking agents is that the films/coatings heat up the surface of the objects they are covering when exposed to UV light continuously. At longer wavelengths, the fluorescent/photoluminescent materials can release the absorbed light as distinctive photon emission. The fluorescence emission spectra of liquid solutions containing CQDs (C1-C4) are displayed in Fig. 6. As compared to the liquid solution’s observed emission spectra, the CQDs show incredibly strong and distinct emission bands in the wavelength range 425 nm. The experimental data provided concrete evidence that the emission peak observed in the visible region could be credited to the fluorescent CQDs. We have performed photophysical analysis on CQDs (C1-C4) as fluorescent moiety in order to comprehend the analogous fluorescence properties of liquid samples. This emission band intensifies with varying conditions of CQDs (C1-C4). Fig. 6 displays the CQDs spectra of excitation and emission in water.

The excitation wavelength for the CQDs (C1-C4) was found to be 341, 340, 340 and 311 nm corresponding to their emission wavelengths at 423,425,429 and 450 nm respectively. As per the observations, it has been noticed that the peak intensity of the PL (Photoluminescence) decreases when the bandgap decreases. This decline is a result of two phenomena. Firstly, there is an increase in nonradiative recombination which leads to a decrease in the PL intensity. Secondly, the thermal motion of phonons causes an increase in their energy, which further reduces the PL intensity (H. Li, Zhu, et al., 2018). The highest intensity fluorescence band suggests that absorbed UV radiation may be converted into the visible region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to characterize the CQDs morphology. The obtained CQDs were found to be spherical, with a wide particle spacing, and virtually no particle agglomeration, as shown in Fig. 7, indicating the good dispersity of CQDs.

The measurements of the samples C1, C2, C3, and C4 revealed that their average diameter falls within the range of 5–6, 9–10, 20–22, and 35 nm respectively. Fig. 7 shows a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the CQDs, which displays the lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of carbon, respectively (Hemmati et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2020). The lattice fringes are orderly and clear, indicating that the preparation of the CQDs was successful. These measurements are indicative of the characteristic appearance of CQDs or colloidal quantum dots, and are consistent with their fundamental structural features (Wen et al., 2023). After the calcination process, the CQDs particles displayed an average diameter of 5 nanometers. However, in the absence of calcination, the average diameter ranged between 10 and 22 nanometers, depending on the experiment conditions. When no treatment was applied to the sample, the average diameter was found to be ∼35 nanometers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface wettability of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The water contact angle is a crucial metric for assessing the wettability of the surfaces of the prepared films. The PVA-based composites’ contact angles are shown in Fig. 8. The CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film have contact angles restricted to a narrow range (16°-24°) because the composite contains a significant amount of hydrophilic groups. On the other hand, the contact angle of the PVA films is 40.36 ± 1.41°, which is higher than that of the PVA-based CQDs films. This implies that CQDs/PVA films are becoming more wettable than PVA films. The distribution, porosity, pore size, and smoothness of the film surfaces are the primary factors that affect the change in contact angle. This is most likely caused by the prepared films’ slightly smoother surface after the addition of CQDs (1 mL), as seen in Fig. 8, and consistent with other published work (Xu et al., 2019). Moreover, the CQDs/PVA films have small contact angles, which results from the absence of newly generated chemical functional groups. This suggests that the addition of CQDs affects the films’ hydrophilicity. The water contact angle decreases from 24.53 ± 0.88° to 16.23 ± 0.20° as the CQDs are varied, changing the surface properties of the blend films and improving the wettability of the prepared films. The outcomes also show that adding different CQDs to the prepared films enhanced their wettability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen transmission rate (OTR) analysis of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen permeability of CQDs (C1-C4) and PVA film was evaluated using the Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) and was analyzed and presented in Fig. 9. The OTR value of pure and commercialized PVA was 6.39 ± 0.64 and 12.45 ± 0.86 cc/m2·day respectively, and the CQDs/PVA composite films varied from 6.39 ± 0.64–0.34 ± 0.03 cc/m2·day. The incorporation of CQDs into the PVA matrix acted as a barrier that effectively retarded the diffusion of oxygen molecules, which resulted in the difference in OTR values. The gas barrier properties of a polymer are influenced by its free volume, which determines the number of gas molecules that can diffuse through the polymer matrix (Alaş et al., 2022). The cross-linking of PVA and CQDs resulted in an enhanced quasi-crystalline structure, which was found to vary based on the experimental conditions. This cross-linking also helped reduce the free volume present in the amorphous region of PVA. As a result, the oxygen permeability was reduced due to the low free volume of CQDs, which in turn improved the oxygen barrier properties. Furthermore, the presence of CQDs in the PVA matrix led to the formation of a dispersed phase, which contributed to the enhanced oxygen barrier properties observed in the CQDs/PVA composite films (Fazeli & Simão, 2018).

According to several research studies, the phenomenon of reduced oxygen permeability of polymer-based films has been observed when incorporating CQDs. This means that the incorporation of CQDs has a significant impact on the oxygen transmission rate of polymer-based films, which is a critical characteristic for various applications such as food packaging, electronics, and medical devices. The findings of the studies suggest that CQDs can serve as an effective barrier material for oxygen-sensitive applications. It was also observed that the samples labeled C1/PVA, C2/PVA, C3/PVA, and C4/PVA exhibited higher OTR values. This enhanced barrier property can extend the shelf life of products and help maintain their quality and freshness, making CQDs/PVA composite films an excellent option for sustainable and effective packaging solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water resistance properties of CQDs (C1-C4)/PVA and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The water resistance properties of the CQDs/PVA composite film were evaluated through a 24-hour water-dipping test to measure weight loss percentage and degree of swelling. The swelling behavior of the PVA/CQDs composite film is shown in Fig. 10. Upon completion of the test, it was observed that pure PVA dissolved in water, rendering the determination of the degree of swelling unfeasible. The CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) composite film demonstrates reduced hydrophilicity because of interacting with CQDs, leading to the consumption of the (-OH) groups of PVA. Consequently, PVA’s ability to bond with H2O diminishes, resulting in decreased degree of swelling (El-Shamy & Zayied, 2020).

The swelling percentage of CQDs/PVA (C1-C4) composite film decreases from 454.23 ± 3.86 % to 181.71 ± 1.79 % with increasing CQDs size due to enhanced rigidification, reduced mobility, and free volume of PVA. Efficient hydrogen bonding between CQDs and PVA matrix decreases the hydrophilicity of PVA, reducing its ability to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules and hence decreasing the degree of swelling of the composites. The weight loss percentage of the CQDs/PVA composite film ranged from 5.6 % to 9.8 %, indicating its water insolubility and suitability for water-based applications.

We have successfully developed a method to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) ranging in size from 5 to 35 nanometers through precise control of synthesis conditions. Our technique involves creating CQDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite films that effectively block UV light. This method is not only straightforward, practical, and cost-effective but also environmentally friendly, as the UV-blocking agent (CQDs) is synthesized from natural green tea. The resulting polymeric film with embedded CQDs effectively prevents UV light penetration. Furthermore, both the PVA films and CQDs are biodegradable, biocompatible, and pose no negative environmental impacts. These CQDs/PVA composite films demonstrate enhanced oxygen barrier properties, which can be utilized to prolong the shelf life of various products while preserving their quality and freshness. By optimizing the experimental conditions in the PVA matrix, the composite films, hydrophilicity, swelling percentage, and OTR values were significantly reduced. The hydrophilicity reduced from 40.36 ± 1.41° to 16.23 ± 0.20° while, OTR decreased from 6.39 ± 0.64 cc/m2·day to 0.34 ± 0.03 cc/m2·day. This decrease in OTR was achieved by varying the experimental conditions of the CQDs in the PVA matrix, which acted as a highly effective barrier, retarding the diffusion of oxygen molecules. This observation is noteworthy as it highlights the potential of CQDs/PVA composite films to serve as a highly effective oxygen barrier in a variety of applications. The water resistance properties show efficient H-bonding between CQDs and PVA matrix which further reduces the hydrophilic nature of PVA. We believe that enhanced oxygen permeability, hydrophilicity and UV-blocking CQDs/PVA composite films have potential applications in various surface-related domains, including paints, food packaging materials and equipment coatings. These films offer effective packaging solutions and could be valuable in a wide range of industrial applications.

Ansari Jamilur R.: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Seo Jongchul: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Park Kitae: Writing – review & editing, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their gratitude to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) for providing financial support through the Brain Pool Fellowship to Jamilur R. Ansari (Grant No.: RS-2023–00263418). The authors also acknowledge Yonsei University, Sinchon Campus (South Korea) for conducting Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) imaging.
